text:
The weekend gets two stars. Would not recommend.

Let us be precise about the problems. Friday night arrives with enormous promise. You shed the week like a coat, make ambitious plans, tell yourself you will rest and also be productive and also have fun. All of these things. You have 48 hours. It feels like abundance.

Saturday morning is fine. Saturday morning is, if we are being honest, the only truly successful part of the weekend. Coffee exists. No obligations yet. A reasonable person might call this pleasant.

Then something goes wrong in the physics. Saturday afternoon disappears into errands, which were not part of the plan but are apparently mandatory. You meant to do something restorative. You went to a hardware store instead. This was not what you wanted.

Sunday is a different product entirely from what was advertised. Sunday presents itself as a day of leisure but is actually a day of anticipatory dread wearing leisure as a costume. You are technically free but psychologically already at your desk on Monday. The laundry must also be done. It is unclear when this became your responsibility but it has always been your responsibility.

By Sunday evening the weekend has simply ended. You did not finish the book. You did not call the person you meant to call. You rested for approximately one afternoon and it was not enough. A week of accumulated fatigue cannot be resolved in two days. The math does not work. Someone ran the numbers poorly at some point in history and the error has never been corrected.

Positive note: the weekend does exist, which is better than if it did not exist. Credit where it is due.

Still, two stars. The duration is the problem. Expand to three days and we can talk. Until then, this is a flawed product with a loyal customer base that has no other options.
